sections.paragraph: an unused root, which seems to have had the sense of nourishing, fattening. Comp. Arab. (r and n being interchanged) فَدَّنَ to fatten cattle, and more frequently بَدَنَ to become fat, thick, بُدْنُ fatness. (In the Indo-Germanic languages compare Futter, English food, fodder, Mediæval Lat. fodrum, and fett, English fat, Icelandic feitr, from the stock foeden, to nourish. The primary root is fad, to which r is added in many forms; compare pita, pater; pigeo, piger, פגר.) Hence-
